items.q: Why is HDI a better measure of development than GNI per capita?
items.correct: HDI combines income, life expectancy and education, capturing quality of life — GNI per capita is just average income and hides inequality and non-economic wellbeing.
items.misconception: Because HDI measures how happy people are.
items.random_wrong: Because HDI is easier to calculate than GNI.
items.why: Students treat HDI as a happiness index. It is broader than GNI but still only captures three measurable dimensions — not subjective wellbeing or environmental quality.

items.correct: Development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs — balancing economic, social and environmental goals.
items.misconception: Sustainable development means stopping all economic growth.
items.random_wrong: Sustainable development means using only solar power.
items.why: Students treat sustainability as anti-development. The Brundtland definition is about balance and intergenerational equity — not zero growth or one specific technology.

items.q: Why is the Brandt Line considered outdated?
items.correct: Drawn in 1980, it predates the rise of NICs (China, India, Brazil) and globalisation — countries like South Korea, Singapore and Chile no longer fit neatly on the 'poor south' side.
items.misconception: The Brandt Line still accurately divides rich and poor countries today.
items.random_wrong: The Brandt Line is the boundary between the Atlantic and Pacific oceans.
items.why: The world has changed: BRICs have grown, some HICs have stagnated. A simple north-south divide cannot capture this. Many geographers prefer the term 'Global North/Global South' loosely or reject the binary altogether.

items.q: Which is an NEE (Newly Emerging Economy)?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Brazil. “United Kingdom” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Income, life expectancy and education. “Only income” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is the approximate UK life expectancy at birth (years)?
items.why: The real figure is 81. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
